scenario.title: Angry recent-purchase complaint
scenario.description: Customer bought a bike two weeks ago and is upset that the rear tire keeps going flat.
scenario.goal: Determine whether the employee de-escalates an angry customer, takes ownership, captures details, and creates a clear inspection or warranty next step.
scenario.customer_persona: Alex, a recent bike buyer who feels let down and needs the issue fixed before a weekend event.
questions_to_ask: What are you going to do about the flat tire?
Am I going to have to pay for this?
Can someone actually look at it tomorrow?

call.transcript:
Scout.ai: Yeah, hi. I bought a bike from you two weeks ago, paid a lot for it, and the rear tire keeps going flat. I've only ridden it on the trail twice. Are you seriously selling broken bikes?
Employee: Uh, I mean flats happen. We can't really know what caused it.
Scout.ai: That's not really an answer. I barely used it and now I can't ride this weekend.
Employee: You can bring it in and we'll look at it.
Scout.ai: Am I going to have to pay for that?
Employee: Depends what it is. If it's not our fault, probably.
Scout.ai: Seriously? I bought it from you two weeks ago.
Employee: Like I said, just bring it in sometime.
Scout.ai: Can someone actually look at it tomorrow? I have an event this weekend.
Employee: Maybe, if we're not too busy.
Scout.ai: Should I leave my name and number?
Employee: No, just come by.
Scout.ai: Fine. Thanks.
Employee: Bye.

report.summary: This was a simple angry-customer test: the caller had just bought a bike, felt let down, and wanted to know whether the shop would make it right. The employee never acknowledged the frustration, sounded defensive about whether the shop was responsible, gave only vague drop-off guidance, and declined the caller's offer to leave contact information. The call ended with no recovered trust, no scheduled inspection, and no owner of the next step.
what_went_well: The employee did allow the customer to bring the bike in for inspection.
The employee did not escalate the tone or argue directly with the customer.
The call exposed a concrete training opportunity: staff need a complaint-handling script for recent purchases.

worst_sentences.exact_sentence: Uh, I mean flats happen. We can't really know what caused it.
worst_sentences.why_it_hurt_conversion: The caller opened angry and worried they had been sold a bad bike. This response defended the shop before acknowledging the frustration, which made the customer feel dismissed.
worst_sentences.better_response: I'm sorry that happened, especially after you just bought it. Let's get it checked and see what is causing the flat.

worst_sentences.exact_sentence: Depends what it is. If it's not our fault, probably.
worst_sentences.why_it_hurt_conversion: The customer asked whether they would have to pay because they were already upset. Leading with possible blame made the shop sound unwilling to help.
worst_sentences.better_response: We'll inspect it first. Since you bought it two weeks ago, we'll check whether it is covered before we talk about any charge.

worst_sentences.why_it_hurt_conversion: The caller volunteered name and phone number. Declining it removed the shop's chance to follow up, reserve time, and recover the relationship.
worst_sentences.better_response: Yes, please. I'll take your name and number, note the tire issue, and make sure the team knows you bought it two weeks ago.

missed_revenue_moments: The caller complained about a bike bought two weeks ago, which was a high-risk retention moment and a chance to protect future loyalty.
The caller said they had an event this weekend, creating urgency that should have been converted into a booked inspection.
The caller offered to leave a name and number, but the employee declined.
report.recommended_staff_script: I'm sorry the tire is still going flat. I would be frustrated too, especially after buying it two weeks ago. Let's inspect it first and see what's causing it and whether it is covered. I can reserve tomorrow at 10:30 or 3:00 so someone looks at it. Can I grab your name and phone number?
report.top_training_priority: Train staff to handle angry recent-purchase complaints with four moves: acknowledge frustration, take ownership, offer a specific inspection time, and capture contact details before ending the call.
